feedback.text: Please consider including provisions for true digital ownership in the Digital Fairness Act. This is especially urgent in the video game industry, where consumer protection and rights are often unclear or inconsistently enforced. Currently, publishers and digital storefronts can revoke access to purchased games without meaningful recourse. Imagine if a book publisher, upon removing a title from their catalogue, came to your home, took the physical copy from your shelf, and destroyed it. This is the digital equivalent of what is happening today. Worse still, publishers can render purchased single- or multiplayer games unusable by shutting down servers or removing online functionality. Games and software that consumers have paid for should remain usable indefinitely. If a publisher no longer wishes to maintain a title, they should be required to provide the community with the necessary tools (e.g. server software) to keep the game alive through private hosting. A Ubisoft executive has stated that consumers should "get comfortable" with not owning their games. This paints a troubling picture for the future of digital media. Nintendo has reportedly blocked internet access on their new consoles for users who have purchased used games. This essentially renders the device nearly useless, raising serious questions about consumer rights and digital access. And this is only the tip of the iceberg when it comes to restrictive practices by major platforms. On a related note, payment processors such as Visa and Mastercard have pressured platforms like Steam and Itch.io to remove entirely legal games by threatening to limit or cut off payment processing services. This practice disproportionately harms small or solo game developers and represents a form of economic censorship. Its also a troubling use of market power to enforce arbitrary content policies. Lastly, gambling-like features in games (such as loot boxes) should be explicitly treated as real gambling. Thank you for considering these points in your efforts to ensure fairness and transparency.
